fault_hypothesis: Lines 69-70 incorrectly remove the last element when both left and right end with the same value, but this duplicate should be preserved. The condition on line 69 causes a spurious pop() that drops one copy of the shared tail value.

spec.expr: not (merged__1 and left and right and merged__1[-1] == left[-1] == right[-1])

constraints.intent: The condition that triggers pop() should never be true for a correctly merged sorted sequence — when both streams share the same last value, both copies must remain.
After initialization, merged must be empty before any elements are appended.
At the end of each loop iteration, the merged list length must equal the total number of elements consumed from both streams.
After the pop() branch executes, merged length is one less than the total inputs — this reveals that the pop() is incorrectly removing an element that should be kept.
The merged result must contain exactly as many elements as the sum of both input lengths.
